schools.summary: Chrysostom: the charge against the Corinthians — many, having learned that not the things which enter defile the man (Matthew 15:11), partook freely of idol-feasts, abusing their knowledge to the harm of the weak; Paul taking up the question of things offered to idols, granting that all have knowledge (that an idol is nothing), but laying down the great principle — knowledge puffs up (inflates with pride, making one conceited and contemptuous of the weak) while charity (love) edifies (builds up the brethren) — the contrast governing the whole discussion (the use of liberty must be guided by love, not mere knowledge), knowledge without love harmful.

themes: we all have knowledge
knowledge puffeth up, but charity edifieth
knowledge without love inflates with pride
love must guide the use of liberty

schools.summary: Gill: whoever has an opinion of himself, or is conceited with his own knowledge, fancying he knows more than he does — which is the case of those elated with knowledge, who treat others with contempt and have no regard to their peace and edification; Barnes: this condemns the vain conceit of knowledge; if any man thinks he knows anything, he knows nothing yet as he ought — the rebuke of conceited, self-confident knowledge (the very pride and contempt of the weak, v.1, proving he does not truly know, for true knowledge is humble and loving, knowing how little it knows and regarding the weak), the right manner of knowing (humbly, lovingly, practically).

themes: if any man think that he knoweth any thing
he knoweth nothing yet as he ought
the conceited proves he does not truly know
true knowledge is humble

schools.summary: Gill: those who love God show it by loving their brethren, being careful not to grieve them, and using their knowledge for edification not destruction; Barnes: there is no true knowledge not connected with love to God, which prompts a man to love his brethren; if any man love God, the same is known of him — the contrast with the conceited knower (true religion not in the knowledge that puffs up, but in the love to God that humbles and edifies), the believer who loves God is known (acknowledged, approved, owned, loved) of him (2 Timothy 2:19, 'the Lord knoweth them that are his', the great blessing far above mere knowledge), love over the knowledge.

themes: if any man love God
the same is known of him
love the mark of true religion
known/owned of God

schools.summary: Gill returns to the subject of idol-meat; Lightfoot renders 'an idol is nothing in the world' — there is no such thing as the god the idol represents; Paul affirming, concerning idol-meat, that an idol is nothing in the world (the heathen deity it represents non-existent, the idol wood, stone, metal, with no divinity) and there is no God but one (the one true God against the many idols) — the foundational truth grounding the believer's liberty (the idol being nothing, the meat offered to it not really consecrated to a deity, and so not defiled), the true knowledge yet to be guided by love (v.7, not all have it).

themes: an idol is nothing in the world
there is none other God but one
the nothingness of the idol
the foundational truth grounding liberty

schools.summary: Gill: there are those called gods by name though not by nature — angels, magistrates, or those men account so; Barnes: the heathen worshipped multitudes, giving them the name of gods, in heaven (the sun, moon, stars, or celestial deities) and on earth; Paul granting that there are many called gods, in heaven or earth — 'gods many, and lords many' (in the heathen reckoning) — the acknowledgment of the heathen pantheon (the multitude of so-called gods and lords the pagans worshipped, named gods though not truly divine), the many false gods set against the one true God (v.6).

themes: there be that are called gods
as there be gods many, and lords many
the heathen pantheon
named gods though not truly divine

schools.summary: Gill: Christians (with the Jews and the best philosophers) hold there is but one God — clear from his perfections, his being the first cause, the governor of the world, and the Scriptures; Paul setting against the many idols the great confession — one God the Father, of whom (the source) are all things, and we in him; and one Lord Jesus Christ, by whom (the agent) are all things, and we by him — the believer's monotheism embracing the Father (the source) and the Son (the agent), the deity of Christ ('by whom are all things' ascribing creation to him, John 1:3; Colossians 1:16), the 'one Lord' not contradicting the 'one God' (the Father and Son one God).

debates.question: Does 'one Lord Jesus Christ, by whom are all things' teach Christ's deity?

sides.position: Yes — it ascribes creation to Christ ('by whom are all things,' John 1:3; Colossians 1:16), setting him alongside the Father as Creator; the 'one Lord' does not divide the 'one God,' the Father and Son being one God

themes: to us there is but one God, the Father
one Lord Jesus Christ, by whom are all things
the confession of the Father and the Son
the deity of Christ

schools.summary: Gill: Paul speaks not of heathens but of Christians, in whom there was the knowledge of these things, but not in all — some, with a conscience (lingering sense) of the idol, still ate the meat as offered to a real idol, and their weak conscience was defiled; not every believer has this knowledge — the weaker brother, whose conscience is not yet clear (still half-believing the idol real), eating against his scruple, is defiled — not by the meat itself (which is nothing, v.4) but by acting against his conscience (the principle that to act against one's conscience, even a mistaken one, is sin, Romans 14:23, the conscience to be respected).

debates.question: How is the weak brother's conscience 'defiled'?

sides.position: Not by the meat itself (which is nothing) but by acting against his own conscience/scruple — to do what one believes wrong is sin (Romans 14:23)

themes: there is not in every man that knowledge
eat it as a thing offered unto an idol
their conscience being weak is defiled
defiled by acting against conscience

schools.summary: Gill: the words may be the argument of those with knowledge (that eating is indifferent, neither meritorious nor blameworthy), or Paul's own sense — that eating or not eating makes a man neither better nor worse before God; meat does not commend us to God (we are neither the better for eating nor the worse for not eating) — the indifference of food in itself (eating or abstaining from idol-meat, or any meat, does not affect one's standing with God, the meat being morally indifferent, it is the heart, faith, and love that matter), setting up the deeper concern of the weaker brother (v.9-13, since the meat is indifferent, the believer can well forgo it for the brother's sake).

themes: meat commendeth us not to God
neither... better... neither... worse
the indifference of food
the deeper concern of the weaker brother

schools.summary: Barnes: this is the reply to the Corinthians' argument (v.8) — though all you say be true, yet take heed, the liberty must not be used to the harm of the weak; Paul warning that, though the food is indifferent, the believer must take heed lest his liberty (real and lawful, the right to eat) become a stumbling-block (an occasion of falling) to the weak — the bounding of liberty by love (the right to eat must yield where its use would cause a weaker brother to stumble, to be emboldened to sin against his conscience, v.10), the real but limited right (free to eat yet should forgo where it harms), the strong responsible for the weak.

themes: take heed
this liberty of yours become a stumblingblock
liberty bounded by love
the strong responsible for the weak

schools.summary: Gill: if a weak believer (not one of equal knowledge) sees one famed for knowledge sitting at meat in the idol's temple, his conscience may be emboldened; Paul illustrating the danger — if the weak brother sees the knowledgeable believer feasting in the idol's temple, his conscience will be emboldened (encouraged, led) to eat the idol-meat against his scruple — the peril of the bad example (the strong, by his liberty openly used, leading the weak to act against his conscience, and so to sin, Romans 14:23), the influence of the strong over the weak (his example emboldens and leads, so he must use his liberty with care).

themes: see thee which hast knowledge sit at meat in the idol's temple
the conscience of him which is weak be emboldened
the danger of the example
the strong leading the weak to sin

schools.summary: Gill: this aggravates the sin of those who, by their example, ensnare weak minds, causing them to stumble and fall, even in some sense so as to perish; Barnes: you will thus be the means of deceiving and destroying him; through the strong man's knowledge (ill-used), the weak brother — for whom Christ died — may perish — the gravity of harming one whom Christ loved enough to die for; the debate on 'perish': the Arminian reading that the weak may truly, finally perish (the danger of apostasy real), the Reformed reading that the sense is the wounding, offense, and tendency toward ruin (the elect not finally lost, yet the sin and danger real, the strong guilty as if he destroyed him).

debates.question: Can 'the weak brother perish, for whom Christ died' truly finally perish?

sides.position: Yes — the danger of final perishing is real; Christ died for him, yet careless liberty may destroy him (the danger of apostasy)
The sense is the wounding, offense, and tendency toward ruin — the elect not finally lost, yet the sin and danger real, the strong guilty as if he destroyed him

themes: through thy knowledge shall the weak brother perish
for whom Christ died
the gravity of harming the weak
the value of the brother

schools.summary: Gill: by sitting at meat in an idol's temple and violating the new commandment of love, the believer wounds the weak brother's conscience and sins against the brethren; Barnes: this shows the evil of causing others to sin; when they so sin against the brethren and wound their weak conscience, they sin against Christ — the solemn truth that to harm a fellow-believer (to wound his conscience, lead him to sin) is to sin against Christ himself (the brethren members of Christ, united to him, so what is done against them is done against him, Matthew 25:40, 'inasmuch as ye have done it unto one of the least of these... unto me'), the supreme motive against the careless use of liberty.

themes: when ye sin so against the brethren
wound their weak conscience
ye sin against Christ
the union of Christ and the brethren

schools.summary: Gill: the conclusion Paul makes for himself, and proposes for the imitation of others — that since the imprudent use of liberty might lay a stumbling-block before weak Christians, he would forgo it; Paul resolving that, if meat make his brother to offend, he will eat no flesh while the world stands, rather than cause his brother to stumble — the supreme example of self-denying love (the apostle willing to give up forever a lawful liberty, eating meat, which is indifferent, v.8, not because it is wrong but for the brother's sake), the love that gladly forgoes the lawful for the brother's good, proposed for the imitation of the strong.

themes: if meat make my brother to offend, I will eat no flesh
while the world standeth
the supreme example of self-denying love
forgoing the lawful for the brother
